title: Friday Sermon 19 July 2002
description:
With reference to the International Jalsa Salana to be held here in Britain next weekend, today’s Friday Sermon is in light of verse 10 of Surah al-Hashr that propounds the significance of hospitality:

For those who had set up home already and had faith to love those who came to them for refuge and hanker not after that which is given them and prefer others to themselves even though they be poor.  Whoso is rid of the greed of their own soul are the ones who will be successful.

('The Holy Quran'.  Al Hashr [The Gathering]: 10).

The Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be on him) said that it is obligatory for one who believes in Allah and the Day of Judgment to be hospitable:

Abu Shuraih al-Ka’bi reported the Messenger of Allah as sayings: He who believes in Allah and the Last Day should honour his guest provisions for the road are what will serve for a day and night: hospitality extends for three days; what goes after that is charity: and it is not allowable that a guest should stay till he makes himself an encumbrance.

(Sulayman ibn al-Ash’ath.  'Sunan Abu Dawood').

There is no blessing for one who is not hospitable.

(Ahmad ibn Hanbal.  'Musnad Ahmad ibn Hanbal').

The Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be on him) said there are great rewards for those who are genial towards their guests and that a small act of kindness like being affable to one’s brother is a virtue and should not be underrated:

Abu Zarr reported: Allah's Apostle ﷺ said to me ‘Do not consider anything insignificant out of virtuous acts even if it is that you meet your brother with a cheerful countenance.’

(Muslim ibn al-Hajjaj.  'Sahih Muslim').

The Promised Messiah (on whom be peace) said that a guest is in a most vulnerable position and one should try one’s utmost that they are not inconvenienced in any way at all.

(Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad.  4 May 1903.  'Malfuzat: iii'.  292).

Speaking specifically about the guests at the he said it is important that they are all treated alike and are provided for all their needs and requirements.*

I expect the host Jamat of Britain to attend the Jalsa all three days unless there is a well-founded reason for not doing so.  I admonish to spend the Jalsa days in remembrance of Allah, to co-operate with those on duty and in-charge, to avoid disagreeable talk, inapt hilarity and a general confrontational attitude.

I reminded of the significance of cleanliness as being integral to our faith, I enjoined ladies to be particular about covering up and everyone to be mindful about restraining of the eyes.  I would stress that the ID cards should be worn at all times.  The Promised Messiah (on whom be peace) had indeed said that this Jalsa should never be considered like ordinary gatherings and spectacles of this world, he had said: This is not some show from among the other worldly spectacles.**  I conclude with a prayer (on whom be peace) for the success and triumph of the Jalsa, Inshallah.

* Ahmad.  25 December 1903.  Ibid, 492.
** Ahmad.  'Shahadat al-Quran' ['Testimony of the Holy Quran'].  (Punjab Press, 1893).  'Ruhani Khazain: vi'.  395.  English: Islam International Publications Ltd, 2021.  154.

The text https://www.alislam.org/urdu/sermon/FST20020719-UR.pdf
